html:
PVC Membrane for Grease and Chemical Exposure

PVC is the membrane we reach for when a roof deals with grease, oil, or chemical exposure that would eat into TPO or EPDM over time. Chile processing plants, dairy-adjacent facilities, and restaurant-heavy retail centers around the valley are where this membrane earns its higher price tag.

Why Grease and Exhaust Exposure Changes the Spec

Kitchen and food-processing exhaust systems vent hot air loaded with grease and fat particulate straight onto the roof surface downwind of the fan. TPO can hold up for a while under light exposure, but sustained grease contact degrades the membrane's surface chemistry faster than plain UV and heat aging alone.

PVC's chemical makeup resists oils and fats in a way TPO's polymer doesn't, which is why national restaurant chains and food processors specify it almost by default. We see this most on buildings near the chile processing operations and dairy facilities scattered around the valley's ag corridor.

Hot-Air Welded Seams, Different Chemistry Than TPO

PVC seams heat-weld similarly to TPO in the field, which means we can verify a good weld with the same probe-and-inspect process. The membrane chemistry is different — PVC contains plasticizers that keep it flexible, where TPO relies on its polymer blend without that additive.

That plasticizer content is worth understanding for long-term planning. Modern PVC formulations retain flexibility far better than older generations of the product did decades ago, but it's still a factor we watch on very long-service roofs, particularly under the UV load this elevation delivers.

We test weld strength with a probe pull on a sample of seams before signing off on any PVC install, the same discipline we apply to TPO, because a weld that isn't fully fused shows the same field symptoms regardless of membrane chemistry.

UV and Heat at This Elevation

High-altitude UV exposure affects every membrane type, and PVC is no exception. Reflective white PVC keeps surface temperatures down the same way white TPO does, which slows the heat side of aging. What PVC adds is chemical resistance the other single-plies don't offer, which is the deciding factor for exhaust-heavy buildings regardless of UV performance.

Wide daily temperature swings still push and pull at PVC seams and flashings the same way they do on any single-ply system, so the fastening and detail work matters just as much here as membrane chemistry does.

Dust, Grit, and Rooftop Equipment Traffic

Food-service and processing roofs tend to carry more rooftop equipment than a typical office building — exhaust fans, grease trap vents, sometimes refrigeration condensers — which means more foot traffic for service and more penetrations for grit to work into over time. We treat walk pad placement and penetration detailing as non-negotiable on these jobs.

  • Chemical and grease exposure source identified before membrane selection
  • Weld quality verified by probe at every seam, same as TPO
  • Penetration count and access path planning around exhaust and refrigeration equipment
  • Reflectivity choice weighed against this building's specific heat gain concerns
  • Warranty terms specific to chemical exposure conditions, not a generic warranty
  • Drain capacity checked against monsoon burst volume for this roof's size

Monsoon Drainage on Grease-Exposed Roofs

A roof with grease buildup near drains can see slower water movement during a monsoon burst than a clean membrane would, since residue collects at low points and around drain strainers. We check and clean drain strainers as part of any inspection on a food-service or processing building, especially heading into monsoon season.

We schedule that pre-monsoon strainer cleaning as a standing item on maintenance contracts for processing buildings specifically, since a clogged drain during a heavy burst backs water up onto the membrane fast, and that's a preventable problem rather than one we should be chasing after the fact.

Cost Against the Alternative

PVC runs a premium over TPO, and we're upfront about that with every owner who calls about a chile processing or restaurant roof. The alternative — installing TPO and watching it degrade early from grease exposure, then repeating the job years sooner than planned — usually costs more over time than paying for the right membrane once. See our 60-mil TPO page for where that membrane still makes sense instead.

What Food-Facility Managers Ask About PVC Roofing

How much more does PVC cost than TPO?

PVC typically runs a noticeable premium over TPO per square, though the exact gap depends on thickness and reinforcement. We price both at bid time so the comparison is concrete rather than a rule of thumb.

Does PVC actually resist grease better than TPO in practice?

Yes — this is one of the most consistent field observations across the roofing industry, and it's why PVC dominates specs for restaurants and food processing regardless of region.

Can PVC be installed over an existing TPO roof that's failing from grease exposure?

Generally not as a direct recover, since the compromised TPO and any wet insulation underneath usually need to come out first. We assess substrate condition before recommending recover versus tear-off.

Does high-altitude UV exposure shorten PVC's lifespan here?

UV and heat affect PVC's aging the same general way they affect any membrane, and reflective color choices help the same way they do for TPO. Chemical resistance is PVC's advantage, not immunity from UV aging.

How often should exhaust-adjacent PVC roofs get inspected?

We recommend more frequent visits than a standard office roof, often twice a year at minimum, specifically to check drain strainers and grease buildup near penetrations.
